query: does NIHSS show bias toward left hemisphere stroke assessment

retrieved_docs.title: Ischemic Stroke -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A neurological examination is essential for all patients suspected of having a stroke. Monitoring vital signs and cardiac rhythm is also critical, as is listening for a neck bruit, which may indicate underlying vascular abnormalities. The National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) is the standard tool used to assess the severity of a stroke. This includes 11 categories, with scores ranging from 0 to 42 (refer to NIH Stroke Scale ). [34] [35] These categories assess the level of consciousness (LOC) through instructions, questions, commands, gaze direction, vision, facial symmetry, motor function of the arms and legs, limb coordination, sensory response, language, speech clarity, and attention to both sides of the body (see Table below). The NIHSS should be administered in the specified sequence, and scoring should reflect the patient’s actual performance during the examination, rather than on predicted abilities.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Cerebral Artery Stroke -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A physical exam is performed as soon as a physician sees the patient. The National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) is a systematic assessment tool that provides a quantitative measure of stroke-related neurologic deficits. NIHSS gives us a brief evaluation of acuity, determination of appropriate treatment, and can predict patient outcomes. It should be completed in less than 10 minutes and ranges from 0 (no deficit) to 42 (maximum). Posterior circulation strokes, including PCA strokes, may be underestimated by NIHSS when compared to anterior circulation strokes. [27] For example, a patient with complete homonymous hemianopsia has only two NIHSS points, but the patient might have a significant infarct in the occipital cortex, and their daily life will be affected drastically.

retrieved_docs.title: Middle Cerebral Artery Stroke -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The most important part of the physical exam to perform in an emergent fashion when there is a concern for a stroke is the National Institute of Health Stroke Scale. This scale is a standardized way of assessing stroke patients to remove subjectivity during their examination. This scale is particularly useful for identifying and localizing strokes involving the anterior circulation, such as the middle cerebral artery, based on the functions they assess. The components include sensation, strength, and coordination in all 4 extremities; production and comprehension of speech, including naming and repetition; visual fields; orientation to self and time; and symmetry and sensation of the face. Large MCA strokes are usually the easiest to recognize of all strokes, as they tend to present with the major deficits that one thinks of when thinking of a stroke, such as unilateral flaccidity, forced gaze deviation, visual field cuts, and, if in the dominant hemisphere, speech deficits. This scale is useful in predicting whether there will be findings on the diffusion-weighted MRI. When performing this exam, it is also important to note whether any abnormalities appreciated are chronic, such as residual deficits from previous strokes. In addition, it is necessary to check vital signs, particularly the patient's blood pressure as hypertension and hypotension can both be associated with neurological symptoms, and blood pressure must be under 180/110 prior to administration of IV TPA. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Cerebral Artery Stroke -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: New guidelines recommend that in patients with acute ischemic stroke within 6 to 24 hours from last known well and who have large vessel occlusion in the anterior circulation, obtaining CT perfusion (CTP), DW-MRI, or MRI perfusion is recommended to aid in selection for mechanical thrombectomy. However, this is only with the strict application of imaging or other eligibility criteria from randomized controlled trials (RCTs) showing benefit in selecting patients for MT. The DAWN trial used clinical imaging mismatch (imaging from CTP or DW-MRI and NIHSS scoring) as criteria to select patients with anterior circulation large vessel occlusion (LVO) for MT between 6 to 24 hours from last known well. The trial demonstrated an overall functional benefit at 90 days in the treatment group (modified Rankin score [mRS] score 0 to 2, 49% versus 13%, adjusted difference 33%, 95% confidence interval [CI], 21 to 44; a probability of superiority greater than 0.999). The DEFUSE 3 trial used perfusion core mismatch and maximum core size as criteria in selecting the patient for MT with LVO in anterior circulation 6 to 16 hours from last time seen normal. This trial also showed outcome benefit at 90 days in the treated group (mRS score 0 to 2, 44.6% versus 16.7%, risk ratio [RR] 2.67, 95% CI, 1.60 to 4.48, p greater than 0.0001). DAWN and DEFUSE 3 are the only trials showing a benefit of mechanical thrombectomy greater than 6 hours from symptoms onset. Only criteria from these trials should be viable for patient selection who might benefit from MT. [21] Clinicians should be aware that most of the patients involved in DAWN and DEFUSE 3 trials had middle cerebral artery occlusions.

retrieved_docs.title: Middle Cerebral Artery Stroke -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Toxic, infectious, and metabolic differentials must be included as well, which is why several laboratory studies must be sent in the evaluation of acute stroke. Sepsis, uremia, hypo and hyperglycemia, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia can all cause symptoms that resemble a stroke. Encephalopathic presentations due to these abnormalities and others can mimic stroke in that they can cause aphasia and apparent deficits in strength, which is why the assessment of laterality is so important in recognizing stroke versus some of its mimics. In these presentations, it can be more challenging to localize a possible central nervous system lesion as the deficits are usually more generalized. In these situations, in addition to the labs that are sent in the acute setting, there will need to be a more extensive laboratory workup, including an infectious workup.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Cerebral Artery Stroke -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Once an acute ischemic stroke is suspected, the standard evaluation includes performing routine airway, breathing, and circulation assessment, checking blood glucose, performing a validated stroke severity scale assessment, and an accurate, focused history regarding the time of symptom onset or last known well or at baseline. The National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) is a standardized method for quantifiable assessment of stroke symptoms. It is the preferred scoring system, and scores range from 0 to 42. A patient with a higher score on this scale is more likely to be considered disabled; however, the definition of "disabling" depends on age, occupation, underlying life-limiting comorbidities, and advance directives.

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Reperfusion Injury -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: During patient assessment, it is essential to gather the patient's past information about when the patient was last known to be well, the onset of symptoms, any relevant risk factors, medications, and other pertinent details suggestive of an underlying condition. Assessing vital signs is crucial, alongside evaluating the level of consciousness, head or gaze deviation, and the laterality of voluntary movements. Ensuring cardiovascular stability is paramount before proceeding with imaging evaluations. In addition, conducting a targeted neurological examination using the National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) is imperative. The neurological examination findings may be categorized using the Hunt-Hess scale or the World Federation of Neurological Surgeons scale. Please see StatPearls' companion resources, " Acute Stroke " and " Acute Ischemic Stroke ," for further information.

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Issues of Concern -- The Mothership or Bypass Strategy
retrieved_docs.content: The eligibility for MT is limited to patients with AIS caused by LVO. Hence, the "mothership" strategy is considered more reasonable if EMS can accurately recognize LVO cases in the field before making a hospital destination decision. To assist in identifying potential LVOs, EMS personnel utilize various stroke scales designed for prehospital detection. These screening tools include the Los Angeles Motor Scale (LAMS), [30] Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Severity Scale (CSTAT), [31] Rapid Arterial Occlusion Evaluation (RACE), [32] and Field Assessment Stroke Triage for Emergency Destination (FAST-ED). [33] Selecting a particular screening tool often depends on regional stroke system preferences and the available resources.

retrieved_docs.title: Ischemic Stroke -- Pathophysiology -- Middle Cerebral Artery Infarction
retrieved_docs.content: Neglect occurs when a patient appears to “ignore” one side of their environment due to an inability to perceive that area. This is a sensory dysfunction associated with the nondominant cerebral cortex. Extinction, or the failure to detect simultaneous stimuli on both sides, is a common bedside test used to assess nondominant cortical lesions.

retrieved_docs.content: Unilateral infarctions of the occipital lobe may cause contralateral homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing.

retrieved_docs.title: Lateral Medullary Syndrome (Wallenberg Syndrome) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the predominant symptoms, the differential diagnosis of LMS will vary. The most common presentation is dizziness and vertigo with nausea and vomiting. The most important differential diagnosis is acute peripheral vestibulopathy, or acute vestibular neuritis. A careful evaluation should differentiate the 2 using the head impulse-nystagmus-skew deviation (HINTS) test. The head impulse test is negative in LMS but positive in cases of peripheral vestibulopathy. Nystagmus is unilateral, away from the side of the lesion with vestibulopathy, but bilateral and/or vertical for LMS. Skew deviation is present in LMS but absent in peripheral vestibulopathy. The HINTS test may identify patients at risk for stroke who require MRI and hospitalization. [27] Hemorrhagic stroke is much less common, and headache is a prominent symptom. Acute demyelination, as in multiple sclerosis, may present with medullary symptoms, but the patients are generally younger women with a history of a demyelinating disease. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Imaging -- Computed Tomography -- Non-contrasted computed tomography
retrieved_docs.content: Non-contrast CT is essential in the management of patients with ischemic stroke. In patients with a large infarction, thrombolysis is not helpful but can increase the risk of post-treatment intracranial hematoma, a severe complication with the risk of brain herniation and death. In this context, different criteria and scoring systems have been developed to exclude the patients with very large infarction from treatment. The most common one is the “Alberta stroke program early CT score (ASPECTS).” In ASPECTS, 10 different areas of MCA territory are evaluated visually on non-contrast CT. For any area with subtle hypodensity, 1 point is subtracted from 10. Patients with an ASPECT score of less than 7 usually demonstrate worse outcomes. [16] (Figure 2) A similar score system has been proposed for posterior circulation (PC-ASPECTS).

retrieved_docs.title: Lacunar Stroke -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Large vessel ischemic stroke in the middle cerebral artery territory is clinically differentiated from lacunar strokes by the presence of cortical signs, including altered mental status, aphasia, and hemineglect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2354
retrieved_docs.content: The arteria basilaris is identified as the anatomical location where transient cerebral ischemia occurs. Transient cerebral ischemia, also referred to by its Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, represents a brief episode of reduced cerebral blood flow that can produce temporary neurological deficits. Recognizing that transient cerebral ischemia originates in the basilar artery is important for clinicians, as ischemic events in this posterior circulation can lead to distinct clinical presentations and influence diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2673
retrieved_docs.content: The basilar artery (arteria basilaris) is identified as a common site where transient cerebral ischemia can occur. Transient cerebral ischemia typically follows a transitory clinical course, indicating that its symptoms are brief and reversible. In Arabic, transient cerebral ischemia is referred to as “نوبة إقفارية عابرة,” underscoring its nature as a short‑lasting ischemic episode.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2126
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is characterized by a transitory clinical course, indicating that the neurological deficits associated with this brief interruption of cerebral blood flow are temporary and typically resolve quickly without permanent damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 916
retrieved_docs.content: الشرايين” (arterial narrowing) is related by an inverse‑isa relationship to “تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي,” signifying that basilar artery narrowing is a particular form of general arterial narrowing. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that asymptomatic basilar artery occlusion, basilar artery embolism, basilar artery narrowing, and basilar artery thrombosis are interrelated expressions of occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries. Recognizing these equivalencies across different languages and clinical terminologies supports comprehensive assessment and management of precerebral arterial pathology, even when patients are asymptomatic, thereby informing holistic patient care and monitoring of general wellbeing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is described as having a transitory clinical course, indicating that its neurological deficits are brief and resolve quickly. This condition corresponds to the Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, which denotes the same short‑lasting, reversible episode of cerebral ischemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2452
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia, referred to in Arabic as نوبة إقفارية عابرة, denotes a brief, reversible episode of reduced blood flow to a focal area of the brain that does not result in permanent tissue damage. This condition is characterized by temporary neurological deficits that resolve within minutes to hours and serves as an important clinical warning sign for the potential development of a full‑blown stroke. Recognizing the relationship between transient cerebral ischemia and نوبة إقفارية عابرة is essential for prompt evaluation, risk stratification, and implementation of preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of subsequent cerebrovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1972
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic occlusion of the basilar artery, a silent cerebrovascular condition, is evaluated in the context of general wellbeing, and the notion of general wellbeing is expressed by the Arabic term “غشي,” which denotes a state of drowsiness or altered consciousness. This linkage underscores the importance of integrating clinical assessment of silent basilar artery occlusion with broader concepts of patient wellbeing and cultural interpretations of health status.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 119
retrieved_docs.content: inverse‑ISA subtype of “embolie; arteria basilaris”; and “تضيق الشرايين” (arterial stenosis) is a broader category that includes “تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي”. Together, these morphological, etiological, anatomical, and linguistic relationships illustrate that diverse cerebral and cardiac infarctions—whether precipitated by embolism, thrombosis, or basilar artery occlusion—are clinical manifestations of underlying precerebral arterial occlusion or stenosis, a framework essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 512
retrieved_docs.content: how basilar artery pathology—including occlusion, stenosis, narrowing, thrombosis, and embolism—can produce both overt cerebral infarctions and brief, transient ischemic episodes, underscoring the importance of recognizing these vascular abnormalities for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2670
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic occlusion of basilar artery, a condition in which the basilar artery is blocked without producing noticeable symptoms, is evaluated in the context of a patient’s general wellbeing, with clinicians interpreting the significance of this silent vascular finding as part of an overall assessment of health status.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral infarction (“الاحتشاء”) results from occlusion, stenosis, embolism or thrombosis of the precerebral arteries, including embolic or thrombotic precerebral artery infarcts and basilar‑artery occlusion, and is pathophysiologically linked to “انسداد الأوعية الدموية” (vascular occlusion). Asymptomatic basilar‑artery occlusion, although sometimes perceived as a state of general wellbeing, may present with transient faintness (“غشي”). Within the hierarchy of nervous‑system circulatory disorders, “Circulatory Diseases and Syndromes of Nervous System” contains the child “حادثة وعائية دماغية” (cerebrovascular accident) and the parent “transient cerebral ischemia” (TIA, “نوبة إقفارية عابرة”), the latter being a brief, reversible reduction in cerebral blood flow. Cerebral artery sclerosis (“تصلب الشرايين المخية”) and intracranial arterial sclerosis (“تصلب عصيدي في المخ”) contribute to chronic arterial stiffening, increase the risk of cerebrovascular accidents, and are associated with

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 916
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic occlusion of basilar artery is understood within the framework of general wellbeing, indicating that this vascular abnormality may be present without overt symptoms but still bears relevance to a patient’s overall health status. This condition is mapped to the broader category of occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, a term that unifies several related concepts across languages and clinical descriptions. The Dutch phrase “Occlusie en stenose van precerebrale arteriën, niet resulterend in cerebraal infarct” is identified as a sub‑entity of occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, emphasizing that such arterial narrowing or blockage can occur without resulting in a cerebral infarct. The Arabic term “انسداد الشرايين” (arterial occlusion) is linked by an inverse‑isa relationship to occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, confirming that they represent the same pathological process. Embolie; arteria basilaris (basilar artery embolism) is also mapped to

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2141
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebraal infarct door embolie van precerebrale arteriën is understood as a specific manifestation that is mapped from the broader condition of occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries. The general category of occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries also serves as the parent concept for Occlusie en stenose van precerebrale arteriën, niet resulterend in cerebraal infarct, which describes cases of precerebral arterial narrowing or blockage that do not progress to a cerebral infarct. The same clinical entity of occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries is expressed in Arabic as انسداد الشرايين, indicating a cross‑linguistic equivalence. Together, these relationships highlight that precerebral arterial occlusion or stenosis can either lead to embolic cerebral infarction or remain clinically silent, underscoring the importance of recognizing both symptomatic and asymptomatic presentations in cerebrovascular assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 139
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral infarction (“الاحتشاء”) encompasses infarcts caused by embolism of precerebral arteries, thrombosis of precerebral arteries, and occlusion of the basilar artery, each representing a manifestation of brain infarction (“احتشاء دماغي”). These events arise from occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, an inverse‑isa of arterial occlusion (“انسداد الشرايين”) and a direct contributor to vascular occlusion (“انسداد الأوعية الدموية”). Occlusion/stenosis of precerebral arteries may present as intracranial arterial sclerosis (“تصلب عصيدي في المخ”) and can involve the intracranial artery structure, sometimes serving as an indirect procedural site for head‑and‑neck endarterectomy. The same disease category includes basilar artery narrowing (“تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي”) and basilar artery thrombosis (“خثار الشريان القاعدي”), which are targets for embolic events such as basilar artery embolism (“embolie; arteria basilaris”), an inverse‑isa of cerebral artery embolism (“انصمام الشريان المخي”). Management of brain infarction often involves anticoagulation with heparin, reflecting the therapeutic role of anticoagulants in embolic or thrombotic cerebral events. The morphological term “الاحتشاء” also applies to myocardial infarction beneath the diaphragm (“احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ تَحْتَ الشِّغاف”), illustrating the shared pattern of tissue necrosis across organ systems. Additionally, “Occlusie en stenose van precerebrale arteriën, niet resulterend in cerebraal infarct” is a parent concept indicating that precerebral arterial narrowing does not invariably lead to infarction, yet remains part of the same hierarchical disease framework. The general concept of arterial narrowing (“تضيق الشرايين”) includes basilar artery narrowing (“تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي”) as a specific anatomical subtype, aiding clinicians in anatomical classification and guiding appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: occlusion or stenosis—whether symptomatic, asymptomatic, thrombotic, embolic, or associated with intracranial arterial sclerosis—is the central pathogenic substrate that leads to cerebral (and occasionally cardiac) infarction, informs diagnostic imaging, and guides interventions such as endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2302
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine with ischemic complication is a broader clinical category that encompasses forms of migraine in which vascular ischemia occurs, and persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction is a specific manifestation within this category. Persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction is also classified as an acute cerebrovascular disease, reflecting its nature as a sudden, stroke‑like event caused by interruption of cerebral blood flow during a prolonged migraine aura. By linking migraine with ischemic complication to acute cerebrovascular disease through the specific entity of persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction, the knowledge underscores that certain prolonged migraine auras can evolve into acute cerebral infarctions, highlighting the importance of recognizing these migraine‑related ischemic complications for timely diagnosis and appropriate stroke‑focused management.
